parents.father_research_notes: Parents not researched. Shared Huntington surname + Norwichtown (Samuel Huntington Sr's town) strongly suggests cousin marriage; possibly daughter of one of Samuel Sr's Huntington brothers.
Parents not researched. Shared Huntington surname + Norwichtown (Samuel Huntington Sr's town) strongly suggests cousin marriage; possibly daughter of one of Samuel Sr's Huntington brothers.

death.cause: Killed in accident while supervising road construction to his estate at Painesville.

parents.father_research_notes: Biological father Rev Joseph Huntington (Y00008). Orphaned as young child after mother's death 1771 and likely shortly after.
parents.mother_research_notes: Biological mother Hannah Devotion (Y00009) died 1771 when Samuel was age 6. Adopted by uncle Samuel Huntington Sr (Y00012), Joseph's brother and Declaration signer.

education.notes: Dartmouth College through junior year; transferred to Yale before completing degree at Dartmouth.

career.notes: Associate Justice 1803; succeeded Return J. Meigs Jr as Chief Justice 1804. Resigned 1808 upon election as Governor.

3rd Governor of Ohio Dec 12, 1808 - Dec 8, 1810. Democratic-Republican. Stormy tenure with controversy over judicial impeachments for upholding judicial review (Huntington would have been impeached as well had he not been elected governor); state capital moved from Zanesville to Chillicothe; Tiffin Resolution terminated terms of all sitting judges; passage of Ohio's 'Blue Laws'; approaching war with Great Britain. Did not stand for reelection. First Ohio governor born in the United States.
Ran for US Senate 1810; lost to Thomas Worthington (later Ohio's 6th governor).

military_service.unit: Western Reserve state militia troops, Northwest Territory
military_service.notes: Appointed Lieutenant Colonel of state militia troops in the Western Reserve by Territorial Governor Arthur St. Clair (also appointed Justice of the Peace by St. Clair).
Appointed Paymaster and Colonel in the United States Army by Gen William Henry Harrison in 1813.

institutions.institution_blurb: Dartmouth through junior year before transferring to Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed supervising road construction at Painesville, 1817
narrative_blocks.body: On June 8, 1817 he was killed in an accident while supervising construction on a road leading to his estate at Painesville OH. He was fifty-one and had just finished his Ohio House term five years earlier. His wife Hannah followed him seventeen months later; both lie at Evergreen Cemetery in Painesville.

narrative_blocks.header: Adopted by uncle the Declaration signer
narrative_blocks.body: Orphaned as a young child after his mother Hannah Devotion died in 1771, he was adopted by his uncle Samuel Huntington Sr — his biological father Joseph's brother, Declaration of Independence signer, and four years later President of the Continental Congress. The adoption made him son of two of the most prominent men in late-eighteenth-century Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded village of Fairport on Lake Erie, 1812
narrative_blocks.body: After losing the 1810 US Senate race to Thomas Worthington and operating a Newburgh grist mill for a few years, he moved to Painesville Township and helped found the village of Fairport on Lake Erie in 1812. The Fairport harbor and its surrounding settlement became one of the early Lake Erie port communities of northeast Ohio.

narrative_blocks.header: Ohio Supreme Court Chief Justice then 3rd Governor
narrative_blocks.body: Elected Ohio Supreme Court Associate Justice April 1803 (the year Ohio gained statehood); became Chief Justice 1804 when Return J Meigs Jr resigned. Elected 3rd Governor of Ohio 1808 — the first Ohio governor born in the United States. His stormy single term saw judicial impeachments, the capital's move to Chillicothe, and passage of Ohio's Blue Laws.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his uncle Samuel Huntington (1731-1796) — Connecticut Governor 1786-1796 and signer of the Declaration of Independence — who would later adopt him after his mother's 1771 death. The uncle is not built as a separate tree entry (out of scope; he has no direct Hooker- or Talcott-line connections of his own).
